title: Money and banking
paperName: Cambridge International A Level Economics (P3 & P4)
summary: 9708 A Level — functions of money, commercial banking, credit creation, and central bank role.
learningObjectives: Explain how commercial banks create credit.

simpleExplanation.title: Money in the economy
simpleExplanation.summary: Banks lend deposits, expanding money supply. Central banks set interest rates and act as lender of last resort.
steps: Money: exchange, store of value, unit of account.
Central bank: base rate, open market operations.
QE: asset purchases when rates near zero.

flashcards.back: Medium of exchange, store of value, unit of account (measure of value).
Fractional reserve banking — new loans create new deposits, expanding broad money supply (subject to reserve requirements).
Lender of last resort, sets base interest rate, controls base money, supervises banking system, inflation target.
Central bank buys/sells government securities to change reserves and influence interbank interest rates.
Central bank creates money to buy financial assets (usually bonds) when conventional rate cuts are exhausted — lowers long-term rates, boosts asset prices.
Rate cut → cheaper borrowing → ↑ C and I → AD rises; also exchange rate channel (lower rates → currency depreciates → ↑ X).

sections.content: **Money and banking** (9.4) supports **monetary policy** essays. Link **interest rates** to **AD** transmission clearly — consumption, investment, exchange rate channels.

items: **Narrow vs broad money:** notes/coins vs deposits and near-money.
**Credit multiplier (simplified):** 1/reserve ratio — actual multiplier smaller due to leakages.
**Liquidity preference:** demand for money — transactions, precautionary, speculative motives.
**QE limits:** may not boost bank lending if confidence low; distributional effects on asset holders.

sections.question:
A central bank cuts its policy rate from 4% to 2% when inflation is 1% and unemployment is above the NAIRU. Commercial banks pass on half the cut to borrowers.

Analyse the likely transmission mechanism to AD and evaluate risks to other macroeconomic objectives. [10 marks]
sections.solution:
**Transmission to AD:**
1. **Effective lending rate** falls by ~1 percentage point (half pass-through) → **cheaper mortgages and business loans**.
2. **Consumption:** lower debt service → ↑ disposable income → **C rises**.
3. **Investment:** lower cost of capital → **I rises** (especially interest-sensitive sectors).
4. **Exchange rate:** lower rates → capital outflows → **currency depreciates** → exports cheaper, imports dearer → **(X − M) improves**.
5. **Combined effect:** **AD shifts right** → output and employment rise toward NAIRU.

**Risks to other objectives:**
- **Inflation:** with inflation already low at 1%, AD stimulus may be appropriate short run; if economy nears capacity, **demand-pull inflation** returns.
- **Asset prices:** lower rates may inflate **house and stock prices** — financial stability risk, worsens wealth inequality.
- **BOP:** depreciation helps X but **import prices rise** — cost-push pressure on inflation.
- **Incomplete pass-through:** if banks hoard margins, **transmission weakened** — policy less effective.

**Judgement:** Cut is justified given unemployment above NAIRU and low inflation, but central bank must monitor **asset bubbles** and **inflation expectations**.

sections.label: Mark a money and banking question

steps.caption: Functions of money: medium of exchange, store of value, unit of account.
Commercial banks — fractional reserve, credit creation.
Central bank controls base money and interest rates.
Quantitative easing when conventional policy exhausted.
